DPG Sindh. Date of hearing: Date of judgment: 11.03.20?5 i 1.03.2015 + T UDGMENT ]an Ali lunejo, fr- This Criminal Revision Application has been filed by the Applicant, Rasool Bux. under Section 439 reacl with Section 435 of the Code of Crimtral Procedure, 1898' challenging the impugned order dated 24'08'2020 passetl by the Learned Special Juclge.\nti-Corruptiou (Provincial)' Karachi (Respondent No. 10), rvherebv the direct complaint filed under Section 200 Cr.P.C. in Special Case No 07 of 2020 was dismissed. The complaint alieged offences under Sections 409' 420,467,468,471,,477-A, and 34 of the Pakistan Penal Code (PPC) read with Section 5 of the Prevention of Corruption '. Act,1947. 2. The Applicant, a legal heir of the late Ahmed Brohj' ciaimsthattireleaselroldr.igirtsofPlotNo'798/799 situated at Chakiwara, Lvari Quarters, Karaclri, were granted to his father in1978. After tl-re death of his father' the Applicant L NIr. Muhammad Arif Khan, advocate along with applicant. a C; lj.l,V) o ILL t REV.APP. NO.179 Or', 202 and other legal heirs sought to distribute the property according to Sharia law. However, Respondent No. 5 (Mst. -. Rabia) and her sons (Respondents Nos. 6 to 9) allegedly refused to release the inherited ProPefty and claimed independent ownership. Tl-re Applicant further a[leges that Respondents Nos. L to 3 (govemment officials) illegally issued a fresh lease in favor of Respondent No. 5, despite the existing lease in favor of the deceased Ahmed Brohi. Additionally, a gift deed was executed in favor of Respondents Nos. 6 to 9, whicl-r the Applicant ciaims was done fraudulently and in coliusion with the Sub-Registrar (Respondent No.4). 3. Tlte Learned Counsel for the {PPlicant argued that the Special'Court failed to follow the proper procedure under Section 200 Cr.P.C. by not calling for 4r'r inquiry report from the Anti-Corruption Establishment or the Investigation Officer' It; was contended that the Special Court disn-Lissed the complaint without applying judicial miuc-l and failed to consider that the Applicant had approached the court after being denied remedy by the concerned authorities.

Additionally, a gift deed was executed in favor of Respondents Nos. 6 to 9, whicl-r the Applicant ciaims was done fraudulently and in coliusion with the Sub-Registrar (Respondent No.4). 3. Tlte Learned Counsel for the {PPlicant argued that the Special'Court failed to follow the proper procedure under Section 200 Cr.P.C. by not calling for 4r'r inquiry report from the Anti-Corruption Establishment or the Investigation Officer' It; was contended that the Special Court disn-Lissed the complaint without applying judicial miuc-l and failed to consider that the Applicant had approached the court after being denied remedy by the concerned authorities. The Counsel argued that the Special Court erred in relying on previous judgments of the apex court, which were not applicable to the facts of the present case. It was further argued that the actious of the Respondents, including the issuance of a Iresh lease and the execution of a gilt deed, were fraudulent and violated the rights of the Applicant and other legal heirs. The Counsel empl-rasized that the Special Court failed to consider the provisions of Article 10- A of the Constitution of Pakistan, which guarantees the right to a fair trial. 4. The Learned APG Sindh and Counsel for Respondents argued that the Applicant failed to follow the proper procedure by not submitting a complaint to the Anti-Corruption Establishment before approaching the Court. It was contended 2 {:) lJ o rt- >lr:S {1 Itr (-1.F tL+ that the disPute is property'rights, and R[V.APP. NO.1 ?9 OF 202 essentially of a civii nature, involving does not fail within the Purview of Anti-Corruption Establishment' The 3 crirtrinal law or the Respondents argued tl'rat the execution of tire gift deed wele done in accordance with the larv, and there was no evidence of I fraud or collusion. The Leamecl Counsel for KMC argued that the Applicant had alternative reniedies available' including the pending civil suit (Suit No' 1342 of 2019), and therefore' the criminal complaint was not maintainable'. He argued that respondents No.1 to 3 are sued through designation' It is not the policy of Iaw to sue any person through designation in criminal proceedings therelore this revisior"r is not maintainable' Lastly, the Ieamed APG and Iearned counsel for Respondents prayed for dismissal of ti-re Criminal Revision Application' 5.

The Leamecl Counsel for KMC argued that the Applicant had alternative reniedies available' including the pending civil suit (Suit No' 1342 of 2019), and therefore' the criminal complaint was not maintainable'. He argued that respondents No.1 to 3 are sued through designation' It is not the policy of Iaw to sue any person through designation in criminal proceedings therelore this revisior"r is not maintainable' Lastly, the Ieamed APG and Iearned counsel for Respondents prayed for dismissal of ti-re Criminal Revision Application' 5. This Court has carefully considered the arguments advance{ by both sides, the impugned order' and the material on record. The learned Special Court observed that the complainant did not adhere to the proper procedure as outlined in the Sindh Enquiries & Anti-Corruption Rules' 1993' Specifically, the complainant approached the court directly-. without 'first filing a complaint with the Anti-Corruption Establishment and obtaining the necessary approval from the comPetent authority to initiate an inquiry' The Special Court stressecl that the complainant could only approach the court directly if the Anti-Corruption Establishment had failed to take action on his complaint. Since the complainant bypassed this mandatory procedural step, the court ruled that the direct complaint was not maintainable' It is an admitted fact that the dispute primarily revolves around a private ProPerty matter' ' specifically concerning tl're leasehold rights of Plot No'798/799 iocated in Chakiwara Lyali Quarters' I(arachi' The compl3inant alleged that the responclents' which include botl-L t' &'I t-. tLb CR.REV.APP. NO.179 oF 202 4 a government officials and private individuals, conspired to issue a fraudulent lease in favor of Mst' Rabia (Respondent No' 5) and executed a gift deed in favor of Respondents Nos' 6 to 9' The court reiterated that tl, e Anti-Cormption Establishment is not authorized to handle complaints related to private ProPerty, disputes. Furthermore, the Applicant himseif admitted in Paragraph No' 12 of the present Revision Application that Suit No. 1342 of 2019 is currently pending before the Court of the leamed Illrd Senior Civil Judge, Karachi-South' As such' the issue of whether the gift deed was obtained through fraud-- or dishonesty by Respondents Nos'1 to 5 must be determined by the Civil Court in its ultimate jurisdiction' In a similar context, the Honourable Supreme Court in the case ofRasool Khan and others tt.

Tlrcre is no bnr on tle potoers of the concenred Court to exercise its iuisdiction wtder section 249-A' Cr'P'C' and acquit an accused.at any stage euer, before ftaming of clmrge' after ffirding an opportu.ni of heaing to tie prosecutor and tlrc accused' if it is found ttmt the clutge is groundless or there is no probability of accused being conoicted and tlut further proceedings in the matter urould be a futile exercise and result in aht'tse of process of Court' In the instant case also, hial Court tolile ncqritting the respondents has exercised its jurisdiction exactly itt accordance tttith lazu which is rightly maintained by tle Higlt Court. It toould be pertinent to note tlmt tlrc respondents luae also resorted to tlrc ciitil renredy and'haue fled cit'il suit in the year 7998 uhich is pending adiudication' hence there utns no justification to.inttoke the iurisdiction of the ciminal Court as tlte aery fact tlut the mutation ruas fraudulently entered or not is yet to be determinecl by the Cittil Cottrt" ' It is also a matter of record that f,}o U vfs + CO UJTr tLq q R.REV.APP. NO.1 79 OF 202 the Applicant seeks the cancellation of the lease for Plot No. 799, Chawkiwata, Lyxi Quarters, Karachi, which was issued in favor of Mst. Rabia D/o Ahmed (Respondent No' 5). Additionally, the Applicant is challenging the declaration of an oral gift conceming the said proPerty.

Rabia D/o Ahmed (Respondent No' 5). Additionally, the Applicant is challenging the declaration of an oral gift conceming the said proPerty. However, such matters fall beyond the scope and jurisdiction of this Court, whether under Section 561-4 of the Code of Criminal Procedure ' (CI.P.C.) or the constitutional jurisdiction provided under Article 199 of. the Cor-rstitution of the Islamic Republic of Pakistan, 1973.hthis context, the Honorable Supreme Court of Pakistan, in the case of Atttit lamal and Others a. Malik Zahoor-ttl-Haq and Othets (2011 SCMR 1023), specifically ! articulated its stance in Patagraph No.7, stating as follows: "7' We haue lreo)d ilrc lenrned counsel and h nzte also perused the record. ln exercise of torit jurisdiction, question of title of a p.roperty cannot be gone into by the High Court. The scope of Article 199 is dependent on the questiotxs Tllxich are dettoid of factual controaersy. A registered instrument can only he cancelled bu n ciztil court of ccmpetent iuri s:lt ction on tlrc q'' 'nd of fraud ': -. o u V otlurwise. Section 39 of the S ecific Relief Act proaides tlut n hich seeks cancellntion of a re?is tered instrutnent has to file a cittil suit bV spproncltin the cioiL court of cotlxpetent.s sdiction and utrit jtLrisdictiott in suclr matters is barred". 6. In the case of Imran-ul-Haq a. Tlrc State (2019 YLR Note t 80), this Court reaffirmed that it is now a settled principle of law that the Anti-Corruption Poiice is neither empowered nor authorized to investigate disputes involving private lands. Furthermore, the Prevention of Comrption Act, 1947 does not permit, tl're Anti-Corruption Police to entertain complaints related to title disputes between private parties. For additional support, reference can be made to the cases of Dilbar Hussain o.

Furthermore, the Prevention of Comrption Act, 1947 does not permit, tl're Anti-Corruption Police to entertain complaints related to title disputes between private parties. For additional support, reference can be made to the cases of Dilbar Hussain o. Habib ur Rehman and another (2077 PCT.LI Note qJ t- -{1 @ o A:!i R.fi[v.APP. NO.1?g Of', 2O2 6 278), Mohammad Moosa a. State t2005 CLC 487), and KhadinL Htrssain a. The State (2017 PCr,Ll 7006).lt is a well-established legal principle that the jurisdiction of the Special Court Anti- Corruption is confined to cases involving corruption by pubtic servants in matters related to government property or public interest. Disputes concerning private property must be adjudicated by the Civii Courts, which have the ultimate jurisdiction in such matters. 7. The impugned order dated 24.08.2020 is both legally sound and well-reasoned. The iearned Special Court correctlv concluded that the compiaint was not maintainable due to procedural defects and a lack of jurisdiction. The complairlant's grievances regarding the allegedfraudulent lease and gift deed sl,ould be comprehensively and exhaustively addressed in the civil suit already fited by him. The impugned order is consistent with established legal principlesandprecedents, and there is no erlor in theSpecial Court's findings or reasoning. 8. In view of 'the above, this Court fiads no illegality, irregularity, or jurisdictional error in the impugned order dated 24.08.2020 passed by the Learned Special Judge Anti- Corruption (Provincial), Karachi. The Criminal Revision Application is devoid of merits and is accordingly dismissed. The prayer to convert this Criminal Revision Application into a Constitution Petition under Article 199 of the Constitution of Pakistaru 7973, is also rejectecl, as the Applicant has adequate remedies available in the pending civil suit. vlsr C o H o o * o 02 \)-.4.F l+o )- iUDGE
